content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement
(916) 322-5662 322-5660 322-5901 322-6441

CONFIDENTIAL

December 7, 1984


Robin B. Wakshull
Deputy District Attorney
County of Santa Clara
270 Grant Avenue
Palo Alto, CA 94306

Re: Your Telephone Request For
Analysis; Our No. A-84-300

Dear Ms. Wakshull:

Pursuant to your telephone request, we have reviewed the
newspaper article regarding the actions of Morgan Hill
Councilmember William H. Brown. It is our opinion that under
the current wording of our regulation 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section
18700, such conduct does not violate the provisions of the
Political Reform Act. However, if the proposed revisions to
this regulation are adopted by the Commission at its January
meeting, such conduct would be prohibited in the future. I have
previously forwarded to you a copy of those proposed
regulations, but I enclose another with this letter and, again,
invite your comments or those of your agency.

Sincerely,

Robert E. Leidigh
Counsel
Legal Division

REL:plh
Enclosures
cc: Judy Davis

• • The Sacramento Bee • Wednesday, May 13, 1987 B5

W. Sacramento water plan decried

By Mike Castro
Bee Staff Writer

Four West Sacramento community leaders are protesting the city's decision to hire an outside firm to oversee the construction of a $18 million water project.

In a special meeting tonight, the City Council is set to award a contract worth about $650,000 to an as yet undisclosed firm.

However, leaders of a years-long fight to convert West Sacramento's system from well water to surface water taken from the Sacramento River met Monday with City Manager Gene Roh to protest the hiring of an outside firm.

The group was made up of Ruby Hulam, chairwoman of People for Better Water, and three former board members—George Kinsight, John Freeman and Carl Landerman of the Community Services District, the area's previous governmental agency.

The four said they believed the Community Services District had left a contract behind that would have given the management of the tract to Frank Clendenen, the engi-

neer who designed the water-treatment system and who worked for years on behalf of improving water supplies for West Sacramento.

Roh could not be reached for comment Tuesday, but Mayor Mike McGowan said the city had decided to hire an outside consultant to oversee construction of the project based upon sound management principles.

City Councilman Fidel Martinez, an attorney, said Roh showed him a copy of the contract the Community Services District signed with Clendenen. That contract did not commit the city to hire Clendenen, Martinez said.

Clendenen could not be reached for comment.

Hulam said Roh had proposed hiring the consultant because Clendenen had underestimated the cost of the water-treatment plant by $2.8 million and couldn't have time to both work on the design of the plant and oversee construction of the water project.

The City Council awarded nearly $15 million in contracts on May 6 for the water-treatment plant and the pipelines.

sections.conclusion: It is our opinion that under the current wording of our regulation 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700, such conduct does not violate the provisions of the Political Reform Act. However, if the proposed revisions to this regulation are adopted by the Commission at its January meeting, such conduct would be prohibited in the future.

sections.question_synthetic: Whether the conduct of Morgan Hill Councilmember William H. Brown, as described in a newspaper article, violates the Political Reform Act under the current wording of California Administrative Code Section 18700.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concluded that the councilmember's conduct does not currently violate the Political Reform Act under existing regulations, but would be prohibited if proposed regulatory revisions are adopted at the January Commission meeting.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: Letter contains clear conclusion but no formal QUESTION section header; question inferred from context. Document contains extraneous newspaper article (Sacramento Bee, May 13, 1987) at end that appears to be OCR artifact or misfiled material unrelated to the FPPC opinion. OCR quality is fair with some scanning artifacts.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether the conduct of Morgan Hill Councilmember William H. Brown, as described in a newspaper article, violates the Political Reform Act under the current wording of California Administrative Code Section 18700.

CONCLUSION: It is our opinion that under the current wording of our regulation 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700, such conduct does not violate the provisions of the Political Reform Act. However, if the proposed revisions to this regulation are adopted by the Commission at its January meeting, such conduct would be prohibited in the future.

embedding.summary: FPPC responds to a telephone request from the Santa Clara County Deputy District Attorney regarding Morgan Hill Councilmember William H. Brown's conduct, concluding it does not violate current law but would violate proposed regulatory amendments under consideration.
